coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a carbon quantum dots-immobilized zirconium-based metal-organic framework composite fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Ji-Min
Hu, Xiao-Wei
Liu, Yi-Xuan
Zhang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic framework-based composite nanomaterials usually exhibit synergetic properties of their components leading to new functionalities and can be used for a wide range of applications. In this study, we designed and synthesized an amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite fluorescence sensor by integrating amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (amine-CQDs) and the zirconium-based metal-organic frameworkUiO-66 using a post-synthetic modification approach (PSMA). Herein, UiO-66 was used as an adsorbent to capture and enrich the target molecules selectively and the amine-CQDs were employed as a functional monomer to selectively and sensitively sense the bonding interactions between UiO-66 and the target molecules, andfurther transduce these chemical events into detectable fluorescence signals. The resulting amine-CQDs@UiO-66 sensor exhibited excellent fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity toward 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) because of the immobilized highly fluorescent amine-CQDs. The fluorescence sensitivity was increased because of the enrichment of 4-NP that originated from the well-defined microporous MOF. Moreover, 4-NP could be detected in a wide concentration range of 0.01–20.0 μM with a detectionlimit as low as 3.5 nM. To the best of our knowledge, this work presents the first combination of the fluorescence functional monomer amine-CQDs with the favorable properties of MOFs by PSMA for selective fluorescence sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites
Nanocrystals
Fluorescent sensor
MOFs,4-nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (amine-CQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Linyi University
Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation , China (Grant No. ZR2018LB05 and ZR2017QB013 ), a project of the Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LC04 ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Linyi University (Grant No. LYDX2016BS072 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite and its application to 4-NP sensing.
PXRD patterns of UiO-66 and the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite, and those simulated from single-crystal XRD data.
(a) SEM image of UiO-66, (b) HRTEM image of amine-CQDs, (c) TEM image of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite, and (d) HRTEM image of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite.
(a) FT-IR spectra of amine-CQDs, UiO-66, and the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite. (b) TGA profiles of UiO-66 and the amine-CQDs@UiO-66composite.
(a) N2adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K and (b) corresponding pore size distributions for UiO-66 and the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite.

Selectivity of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66-based sensor for 4-NP over other analogs and ions. The concentrations of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 and metal ions were 40 μg mL−1 and 10 μM, respectively. Excitation wavelength, 365 nm; silt widths for excitation and emission, 2.5 and 5 nm.
FL emission spectra of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 in the presence of different concentrations of 4-NP.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a carbon quantum dots-immobilized zirconium-based metal-organic framework composite fluorescence sensor for highly sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Linyi University, Linyi, 276005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic framework-based composite nanomaterials usually exhibit synergetic properties of their components leading to new functionalities and can be used for a wide range of applications. In this study, we designed and synthesized an amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite fluorescence sensor by integrating amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (amine-CQDs) and the zirconium-based metal-organic frameworkUiO-66 using a post-synthetic modification approach (PSMA). Herein, UiO-66 was used as an adsorbent to capture and enrich the target molecules selectively and the amine-CQDs were employed as a functional monomer to selectively and sensitively sense the bonding interactions between UiO-66 and the target molecules, andfurther transduce these chemical events into detectable fluorescence signals. The resulting amine-CQDs@UiO-66 sensor exhibited excellent fluorescence selectivity and sensitivity toward 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) because of the immobilized highly fluorescent amine-CQDs. The fluorescence sensitivity was increased because of the enrichment of 4-NP that originated from the well-defined microporous MOF. Moreover, 4-NP could be detected in a wide concentration range of 0.01–20.0 μM with a detectionlimit as low as 3.5 nM. To the best of our knowledge, this work presents the first combination of the fluorescence functional monomer amine-CQDs with the favorable properties of MOFs by PSMA for selective fluorescence sensing.

We fabricated amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites via a post-synthetic modification approach, incorporating amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots functional monomers that could respond to the analyte 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). We found it to be highly selective and sensitive for 4-NP, detecting this species even in mixtures of metal ions and other phenols.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are inorganic-organic hybrid materials comprising unique combinations of metal ions and organic ligands. They have generated considerable interest as advanced porous materials because of their high surface area, uniform pore size distribution, and good chemical and thermal stabilities [1–4]. Most researchers have focused on the rational design and construction of MOFs that exhibit permanent porosity for applications in gas storage/separation [5,6], heterogeneous catalysis [7], and toxic compound adsorption and separation [8,9]. However, their application in chemical sensing has received scant attention so far.

The development of MOFs for sensing applications is difficult because effective signal transduction in these systems is very challenging [10]. Recently, MOFs with intrinsic luminescence have been used as chemical sensors to detect metal ions, organic molecules, and explosives. However, applications of these luminescent MOFs are limited by their low quantum yields, which are a result of either the poor intrinsic luminescence of inorganic or organic components, or the luminescence originating from the weak metal to ligand charge transfer. Most MOFs tend to emit negligible luminescence or show a considerable luminescence response to only a few types of guests (e.g., good electron donors or acceptors). These factors severely limit their application in a real world scenario [11]. Therefore, introducing luminescent guest materials with high quantum yields into the channels of MOFs is an important alternative approach to producing ideal luminescent MOF-based sensory materials while retaining the original MOF framework and properties. The post-synthetic modification approach (PSMA) is an alternative strategy for the construction of functionalized MOF-based composites, in which functional materials are introduced and immobilized in the channels of MOFs. Thus, new physical and chemical properties, as well as enhanced performance that would not be attainable by the individual components, may be accessed. Recently, several researchers have reported that loading other functional materials into MOFs by PSMA can produce materials that exhibit enhanced or unique properties. For example, the adsorption capacity of UiO-66 has been improved by loading it with phosphate moieties [12], the catalytic performances of MOFs have been improved by incorporating metal nanoparticles [13,14], and the fluorescence sensing of MOFs has been improved by incorporating Eu(III) or Tb(III) ions [15,16].

MOF-based fluorescence sensors have been previously developed by loading lanthanide ions (such as Ln3+, Eu3+, and Tb3+) into MOFs [17–20] or introducing quantum dots into MOF nanoparticles using a one-pot method [21–27]. These materials have been used for sensing metalions, anions, and organic small molecules. Fabricating MOF-based composites with excellent fluorescence performances is challenging but important for extending the applicability of these hybrid materials to fluorescence sensors for highly sensitive detection of toxic compounds. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted attention for their unique properties such as high photoluminescence stability, facile and environmentally friendly synthesis, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and inherent selectivity for target molecules, leading to promising applications in fluorescence sensors [28,29]. Zr6O4(OH)4(bdc)6 (bdc = benzene-1,4-dicarboxylate), denoted as UiO-66, is a representative porous MOF. This MOF is composed of a zirconium inorganic brick structure [Zr6O4(OH)4(CO2)12] linked with twelve terephthalate ligands. The MOF is built up from octahedralcages with a diameter of 1.1 nm and tetrahedral cages with a diameter of 0.75 nm, which afford it a large surface area, large-pore channel, exceptional pore volume, and high thermal stability [30,31]. 4-Nitrophenol (4-NP) is a common intermediate formed during the production of pharmaceuticals, dyes, and leather products [32]. However, 4-NP is also recognized as a toxic pollutant that causes significant damage to human health and the environment [32]. Therefore, highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-NP is of great significance for public health and environmental protection.

Herein, we report a facile strategy to immobilize amine-functionalized carbon dots (amine-CQDs) in the channels of UiO-66 for highly sensitive and selective detection of 4-NP via post-synthetic modification approach. The selectivity and sensitivity were improved in two ways; first, the UiO-66 composite was used to capture and enrich the target molecules selectively, and second, fluorescent (FL) amine-CQDs were used directly as functional monomers to sense the bonding interactions between UiO-66 and the target molecules. Thus, a rapid, selective, and sensitive sensing method based on the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 FL probe was developed for the detection of trace 4-NP over a linear region of 0.01–20.0 μM at a low detection limit of 3.5 nM. This development suggests promising applications of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite in the field of toxic compound analysis.

Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4, 98%), trinitrophenol (TNP, 98%), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP, 98%), 4-nitrophenol (4-NP, 98%), and phenol (PHE, 98%) were purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, 30 K), acetic acid (HAc, 99.5%), citric acid (CA, 98%), ethylenediamine (EDA, 98%), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%) were obtained from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 98%) was purchased from Sinapharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytically pure and used as received without further purification.

In a typical experiment, a 15 mL DMF solution of H2BDC (0.13 mmol) was mixed with ZrCl4 (0.13 mmol), PVP (10 mg), and HAc (20 μL) at room temperature. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was sealed and kept at 120 °C for 24 h. The reaction vessel was then removed from the oven and allowed to cool to room temperature. The resulting product was isolated by centrifugation, washed five times with DMF, and dried in vacuum at 120 °C for 4 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (amine-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Amine-CQDs were prepared by a modified version of a previously reported procedure [28,29]. In a typical preparation, 0.5 go f CA and 5.0 mL of EDA were dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water. The solution was then poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, which was sealed and maintained at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting amine-CQDs were dialyzed for three days (molecular porous membrane tubing, cutoff 1000) at room temperature to remove the unreacted moieties.

UiO-66 nanocrystals (100 mg) were immersed in the presence of amine-CQDs (1 mL) for 12 h at room temperature. The precipitates were washed thoroughly several times with deionized water under ultrasonication and centrifugation. The products were then activated in vacuum at 100 °C for 5 h.

Powder X-ray diffraction(PXRD) data were obtained on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) at room temperature. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were measured on a field emission scanning electron micro-analyzer (Hitachi S-4800) operated at an accelerating voltage of 5 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. UV–Vis spectra were collected on a UV-1800 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded in the range 400–4000 cm−1 on a Bruker Vector22 FT-IR spectrophotometer using KBr pellets. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Mettler-Toledo (TGA/DSC1) thermal analyzer under nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Nitrogen sorption experiments were performed at 77 K on a Belsorp-max volumetric gas sorption instrument. Surface areas were determined by the Langmuir equation. Ultrahigh purity N2 was used in the adsorption study. A Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer was used for fluorescence measurements.

FL spectra were measured on a Hitachi FL-7000 spectrophotometer equipped with a quartz cell at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm with excitation and emission slit widths of 2.5 and 5 nm. To evaluate the specific recognition performances of the as-obtained materials, 4-NP was added into 4.0 mL of 40 μg mL−1 probe solution incrementally to final concentrations ranging from 10 nM to 20 μM. The FL intensities were recorded and plotted.

The synthetic procedure of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Blue fluorescent amine-CQDs were first prepared by mixing citric acid and ethylenediamine in deionized water, as previously reported [29]. Then, UiO-66 nanocrystals were obtained by mixing DMF solutions of ZrCl4, H2BDC, PVP, and HAc (20 μL). Finally, the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite was synthesized by exposing UiO-66 to an aqueous solution containing amine-CQDs using PSMA. The FL emission spectra of amine-CQDs and amine-CQDs@UiO-66 were measured (Fig. S1). Amine-CQDs showed a maximum FL emission at 440 nm and a strong blue FL when excited at 365 nm (Fig. S2). The FL spectrum of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 showed a slight red shift but still displayed blue FL. However, for pure UiO-66, there was no obvious peak at 444.5 nm, suggesting that the blue FL of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 originated from the amine-CQDs rather than the UiO-66 nanocrystals. Thus, the PL properties of the amine-CQDs in UiO-66 were retained. The FL stability of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 was also investigated by monitoring its FL intensity every day. After storage for 10 days, no obvious FL quenching was observed (Fig. S3), implying good stability of the as-prepared amine-CQDs@UiO-66 fluorescence sensors.

Fig. 2 shows the powder XRD patterns of the UiO-66 nanoparticles and amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites. It can be clearly seen that all the characteristic diffraction peaks of the as-prepared samples were good in accordance with those simulated from single crystal data. The powder XRD patterns of the resulting amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites agreed well with those of the pristine UiO-66 nanoparticles [30], indicating that the composite framework was not destroyed during the post-synthetic loading treatment (Fig. 2). The morphology of UiO-66 was investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy, which revealed that UiO-66 existed as mono disperse nanoparticles with cubic shape and a particle size of ∼80 nm (Fig. 3 a). The amine-CQDs and amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites were investigated by TEM, as shown in Fig. 2b–d, which revealed some spherical amine-CQDs with diameters of ∼1 nm (Fig. 3b). The TEM results also indicated that the UiO-66 nanocrystals were cubic, which was consistent with the SEM results (Fig. 3c). The amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite synthesized in this study exhibited the same morphology as that of UiO-66, having a well-defined core-shell structure containing multiple amine-CQDs (Fig. 3d and Fig. S4). The pore channels of UiO-66 nanocrystals were clearly present.

The FT-IR data further confirmed the chemical composition of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite (Fig. 4 a). The characteristic bands at around 1576 and 1389 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of νas(-COOˉ) and νs(-COOˉ) of the carboxylate group in BDC2−. For as-prepared UiO-66, an additional absorption peak was observed at 1661 cm−1, which was attributed to C=O stretching in DMF. The band around 1661 cm−1 disappeared in the spectrum of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 because the amine-CQDs nanocrystals replaced the coordinated DMF molecules and entered the pore channels of the framework, indicating that the amine-CQDs nanocrystals were immobilized in the framework. Fig. 4b shows the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the as-prepared UiO-66 and amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites, which displayed two main weight loss steps. The first weight loss in the temperature range of 30–420 °C was attributed to the loss of guest molecules. Most of the solvent molecules in the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite were lost at 120 °C, while those for UiO-66 were removed at 300 °C. This loss indicated that most of the DMF guest molecules were replaced by amine-CQDs in the composites. The second major loss in the temperature range from 420 to 650 °C was attributed to the decomposition of the framework; the weight losses occurred at the same temperature for UiO-66 and amine-CQDs@UiO-66 nanocrystals, indicating that the thermal stability of the frameworks was not influenced by post-synthetic loading. The major difference in the TGA profiles was that the weight losses in both steps were smaller for the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite than for UiO-66. For the first step, the difference was attributed to the smaller pore size and volume of the as-prepared amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites compared to those of UiO-66, which implied the existence of fewer guest solvent molecules in the framework. For the second step, the residual solids contained amine-CQDs. Therefore, there were more residual solids in the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite than in the UiO-66 nanoparticles. Thus, the TGA curves further confirmed that amine-CQDs were immobilized in the framework.

The porosity of MOFs was investigated by measuring N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K (Fig. 5 ). Amine-CQDs@UiO-66 showed a BET specific surface area of 569.7 m2 g−1, which was much smaller than that of pure UiO-66 nanocrystals (981 m2 g−1). The average channel size of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 (0.58 nm) was also smaller than that of UiO-66 (0.61 nm). This difference was possibly because the amine-CD nanocrystals lined the pore channels of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites, resulting in a decreased micropore volume and channel diameter. Thus, the N2 sorption measurements confirmed the successful completion of the loading process.

The dose response of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 to 4-NP was found to be closely linked with the concentration of amine-CQDs@UiO-66, pH, and response time because the concentration of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 affected the quenched FL intensity of 4-NP. The initial FL intensity (in the absence of 4-NP) and the quenched FL intensity (in the presence of 4-NP) of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite were both pH-dependent, and together with the response time, key factors for potential applications. Experimental results indicated that an amine-CQDs@UiO-66 concentration of 40 μgmL−1 and a pH of 7.0 were suitable, and that 5 min was sufficient time for generating a response (Figs. S5–S7). The quenching kinetics achieved a plateau within 5 min. The rapid response speed was attributed to the microporous structure and the high surface area of amine-CQDs@UiO-66.

In general, nitrophenol analogs and metal ions can also cause FL quenching of amine-CQDs@UiO-66. Thus, the selectivity of this amine-CQDs@UiO-66 sensing system was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6 , the effects of other nitrophenolanalogs besides 4-NP(e.g., PHE, DNP, and TNP), and other cations such as Al3+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ba2+,Co2+,Ag+, K+, Na+, and NH4 + on the FL response of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 were investigated using the same concentration of 10 μM. The FL response (F0/F–1) of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 to 4-NP (>1.55) was much higher than that for nitrophenol analogs and others ions (<0.4), except in the cases of DNP, TNP, Cu2+, and Ag+. These species showed slight FL quenching because of their stronger affinity constants with the amino group of amine-CQDs, although this quenching was insignificant. The other metal ions and PHE caused no obvious FL changes in amine-CQDs@UiO-66. The high selectivity was attributed to the selective adsorption of phenol compounds by UiO-66 and the selective sensing of 4-NP by amine-CQDs, which has been proven by comparative tests (Fig. S8). When different phenol compounds were added to the amine-CQDs solution (each at same concentration), PHE caused almost no FL quenching. However, the FL-quenching efficiency of TNP was higher than that of PHE, 4-NP, and DNP. This is because TNP contains more nitro groups than PHE, 4-NP, and DNP, allowing electrons to be transferred easily from the amino group on the amine-CQDs surface to the aromatic rings of the phenol compounds. The adsorption capacity of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 was 17.3 mg g−1, the initial 4-NP concentration was 20 mg L−1, and the maximum adsorption capacities for DNP and TNP were 1.8 and 1.3 mg g−1, respectively. The considerably higher adsorption capacity of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 nanocomposite for 4-NP is probably a result of the linear structure and small size of 4-NP in comparison to those of DNP and TNP. Although the FL-quenching efficiency of TNP and DNP in a single amine-CQDs solution was higher than that of 4-NP, DNP and TNP were hardly adsorbed by the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite compared to the adsorption of 4-NP. Therefore, the FL quenching of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 was in response to the high selectivity for 4-NP. In addition, since the metal ions have much smaller diameters than those of the channels of amine-CQDs@UiO-66, they can easily enter and exit the channels of amine-CQDs@UiO-66. Since there were no specific interactions between the functional monomer and metal ions in the recognition sites, any quenching from metal ions could be neglected. Therefore, amine-CQDs@UiO-66 is an ideal candidate for developing a highly selective and ultrasensitive sensor for 4-NP.

The sensitivity, linear response range, and detection limit of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 sensing system were measured under optimized experimental conditions. FL quenching titrations were performed by adding amine-CQDs@UiO-66 to aqueous solutions of 4-NP with different concentrations, and a dose-dependent interaction between the FL response (F0/F) and 4-NP concentration was found. As shown in Fig. 7 , the FL intensity decreased with the increasing concentration of 4-NP over a wide concentration range of 0.01–20 μM. This relationship can be quantified by a modified Stern-Volmer equation [11]:
(1)
F 0
F
=
K
sv
C + 1 ,
where F0 and F are the FL intensities of the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 in the absence and presence of 4-NP, respectively, and C is the concentration of 4-NP. From the linear plot of Eq. (1), the Ksv value of 0.16 7for 4-NP was calculated with a correlation coefficient of 0.990. The theoretical lower detection limit of amine-CQDs@UiO-66 for 4-NP was calculated as 3.5 nM (3 S/N). In contrast to the other reported detection methods for 4-NP, the amine-CQDs@UiO-66 sensing system showed higher sensitivity toward 4-NP, which was attributed to the highly selective recognition effect of the FL amine-CQDs and the excellent absorption ability of the MOFs.

Herein, a facile synthetic method of UiO-66 nanoparticles under solvothermal conditions has been reported. Amine-CQDs were immobilized on these MOF particles using a post-synthetic Modification approach to afford a FL-functionalized amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composite. The FL-functionalized amine-CQDs@UiO-66 composites were found to be excellent FL probes for 4-NP detection. The synthesized FL-functionalized MOFs exhibited improved FL selectivity and sensitivity compared to the free amine-CQDs in solution because of selective adsorption and accumulation of the target molecule in the pore channels of the MOFs, in addition to the specific interaction between the functional monomer amine-CQDs and 4-NP in the recognition sites. We believe that the material design and synthesis strategy reported here is useful for the development of other functionalized MOF-based composite materials for various applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (Grant No. ZR2018LB05 and ZR2017QB013), a project of the Shandong Province Higher Educational Science and Technology Program (Grant No. J16LC04), and the Natural Science Foundation of Linyi University (Grant No. LYDX2016BS072).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2018.07.042.

Controlled growth and gas sorption property of IRMOF-3 nano/microcrystals

Imparting tunable and white-light luminescence to a nanosized metal-organic framework by controlled encapsulation of lanthanide cations

A novel luminescent monolayer thin film based on post-synthetic method and functional linker

Visual enantio selective probe based on metal organic framework incorporating quantum dots
Synthesis and applications of metal-organic framework-quantum dot (QD@MOF) composites

Metal-organic framework from CQD quantum dot incubated luminescent metallohydrogel
